title: The Growing Risks of Private Credit Markets
source: The Guardian - The Economist (Web Edition)  04 April 2026
content:
Private credit promised high returns to investors and safety to financial regulators, but the reality is becoming increasingly concerning. Over the past two decades, massive private-equity houses have shifted from buying and selling companies to lending to them directly. This lending often funds private-equity deals, and roughly a third of these private loans sit in funds open to individual investors. Recently, investors have become spooked by the realization that many loans are tied to software firms vulnerable to disruption. Consequently, redemption requests at big funds have surged, forcing managers to strictly enforce withdrawal limits to prevent a rapid collapse.

While failing private-credit funds are not about to bring down the entire financial system, their structural issues are troubling. These funds are relatively simple and have low leverage, allowing them to hobble along by rationing capital withdrawals over time. However, the bigger concern is the exposure of insurance companies, which hold complex securitized products tied to buyout debt. The kings of private markets have seen their share prices plummet, reflecting their excessive exposure and reliance on asset-gathering fees. Moving forward, these massive private-equity firms will face intensified competition from deregulated banks and a warier retail investor base.

As the woes of private-credit funds intensify, they threaten to raise the cost of borrowing for firms across the broader economy. The cost of debt in public markets has already risen, further burdened by the massive debt issued to fund the data-center boom. If a wave of defaults occurs, it could severely slow the adoption of the very technologies that fueled recent economic growth. Balancing the need for cheap liquidity with the realities of private market valuations remains an irreconcilable challenge. Ultimately, the humbling of private credit highlights the hidden costs that these alternative lending structures can impose on the financial system.
